exercises.title: Eureka startup — what gets registered?
exercises.scenario: Services register with Eureka using spring.application.name.
exercises.instructions:
Three Spring Boot services start up. Each has:

```properties
# market-service:
spring.application.name=market-service
server.port=8081
eureka.client.service-url.defaultZone=http://localhost:8761/eureka/

# order-service:
spring.application.name=order-service
server.port=8082
eureka.client.service-url.defaultZone=http://localhost:8761/eureka/

# eureka-server:
spring.application.name=eureka-server
server.port=8761
eureka.client.register-with-eureka=false
```

What appears in the Eureka dashboard at http://localhost:8761?
If a Feign client in order-service is @FeignClient(name='market-service'), which host:port does it call?
exercises.expected_solution:
Eureka dashboard shows:
- MARKET-SERVICE: 1 instance at host:8081
- ORDER-SERVICE: 1 instance at host:8082
(eureka-server itself does NOT appear — register-with-eureka=false)

@FeignClient(name='market-service') resolves to:
→ http://host:8081 (the registered IP/port of market-service)
Eureka provides the actual host; Feign + LoadBalancer use it to make the HTTP call

test_cases.expected:
Eureka dashboard shows:
- MARKET-SERVICE: 1 instance at host:8081
- ORDER-SERVICE: 1 instance at host:8082
(eureka-server itself does NOT appear — register-with-eureka=false)

@FeignClient(name='market-service') resolves to:
→ http://host:8081 (the registered IP/port of market-service)
Eureka provides the actual host; Feign + LoadBalancer use it to make the HTTP call

hints: register-with-eureka=false: server doesn't register itself
Each service registers under its spring.application.name
@FeignClient(name=...) must match the registered service name

exercises.title: Complete Eureka server application.properties
exercises.scenario: Eureka server needs specific properties to not register with itself.
exercises.instructions: Fill in the application.properties for a Eureka server.
exercises.starter_code:
server.port=____
spring.application.name=eureka-server

# Don't register server with itself:
eureka.client.register-with-eureka=_____
eureka.client.fetch-registry=_____

# Disable self-preservation in development:
eureka.server.enable-self-preservation=_____

blanks.hint: Server should not register with itself
Server should not fetch registry from itself
Disable in dev to avoid zombie registrations

hints: 8761: conventional Eureka server port
register-with-eureka=false: prevents server registering with itself
self-preservation=false: disable in dev to avoid stale entries

exercises.title: Eureka Server minimal setup
exercises.scenario: Three lines to stand up a complete Eureka service registry.
exercises.instructions:
Write a complete minimal Eureka Server:
1. `EurekaServerApplication` with @SpringBootApplication + @EnableEurekaServer
2. application.properties with all required server config
3. Maven dependency snippet
hints: @EnableEurekaServer: activates the Eureka registry
register-with-eureka=false: Eureka server doesn't join its own registry
Dashboard at http://localhost:8761 after startup

exercises.title: Register a service with Eureka
exercises.scenario: Adding Eureka client to an existing Spring Boot microservice.
exercises.instructions:
Modify `market-service` to register with Eureka:
1. Application class with @EnableDiscoveryClient
2. Complete application.properties with Eureka client config
3. Heartbeat interval: 10 seconds, lease expiry: 30 seconds
4. Show the Maven dependency to add
hints: @EnableDiscoveryClient: registers this service with Eureka on startup
spring.application.name: the name shown in Eureka dashboard
instance-id with port: allows running multiple instances locally

exercises.title: Feign without url= — service discovery
exercises.scenario: Remove url= from @FeignClient to use Eureka discovery.
exercises.instructions:
Show the before/after for MarketServiceClient:
1. BEFORE: @FeignClient with hardcoded url property
2. AFTER: @FeignClient with only name= (Eureka discovers the URL)
3. What additional dependency is needed for load balancing?
4. What must match between the Feign client name and market-service?
hints: Remove url= entirely: Feign uses Eureka for discovery
spring-cloud-starter-loadbalancer: picks one instance from the Eureka list
@FeignClient(name=) must equal spring.application.name (case-insensitive)

exercises.title: DiscoveryClient — list registered instances
exercises.scenario: DiscoveryClient programmatically queries the Eureka registry.
exercises.instructions:
Write a `ServiceDiscoveryController` that:
1. @Autowired DiscoveryClient
2. GET /discovery/services → List<String> of all registered service names
3. GET /discovery/services/{name} → List of service instance URIs for that service
4. GET /discovery/services/{name}/count → instance count
hints: DiscoveryClient.getServices(): all registered service names
DiscoveryClient.getInstances('name'): List<ServiceInstance> for that service
instance.getUri(): the base URI (http://host:port)

exercises.title: Load balancing observation — round-robin across instances
exercises.scenario: Two instances registered; requests alternate between them.
exercises.instructions:
Write a `LoadBalancedClient` @Component that:
1. Injects a Feign client WITHOUT url= (uses Eureka)
2. Calls the service 6 times
3. Each service instance logs its port: System.out.println('Request handled by port: ' + serverPort)
4. Expected: alternating between the two instances (round-robin)
5. Show the application.properties needed to run two instances
hints: @Value('${server.port}') int port: shows which instance handled the request
instance-id=name:port: prevents instance collision running 2 instances locally
@PostConstruct: runs after Feign client is injected and ready

exercises.title: Complete three-service setup with Eureka
exercises.scenario: Eureka server + two microservices discovering each other.
exercises.instructions:
Write the complete configuration for a three-service setup:
1. `eureka-server` (port 8761) — service registry
2. `product-service` (port 8081) — registers with Eureka
3. `order-service` (port 8082) — registers with Eureka + calls product-service via Feign

For each: Application class + application.properties. Feign client without url=.
hints: Start order: eureka-server → product-service → order-service
All three need spring-cloud-dependencies BOM in dependencyManagement
LoadBalancer dependency required when @FeignClient has no url=

exercises.title: @SpringBootTest with Eureka — use @EnableDiscoveryClient(autoRegister=false)
exercises.scenario: Disable Eureka registration in tests to avoid failures.
exercises.instructions:
Write a @SpringBootTest for a service that has Eureka configured. Show how to:
1. Disable Eureka registration in tests (don't start Eureka just for tests)
2. Use WireMock for downstream service HTTP calls
3. Override the Feign client URL with @TestPropertySource to point to WireMock
4. Still test the business logic end-to-end
hints: eureka.client.enabled=false: disables Eureka registration in tests
@TestPropertySource: test-only property overrides
Alternatively: @MockBean FeignClient replaces Feign with Mockito mock

exercises.title: Eureka heartbeat — what happens when a service dies?
exercises.scenario: Eureka deregisters services that stop sending heartbeats.
exercises.instructions:
market-service is running and registered with Eureka. The process is killed (no graceful shutdown). Eureka lease settings:
- lease-renewal-interval-in-seconds=10
- lease-expiration-duration-in-seconds=30

Describe the timeline:
- t=0: market-service killed
- t=10s: ?
- t=30s: ?
- t=31s: order-service calls Feign marketClient.getQuote() — what happens?
exercises.expected_solution:
t=0: market-service killed — stops sending heartbeats
t=10s: Eureka expects heartbeat (lease-renewal-interval) — none arrives, marks as missed
t=30s: Lease expiration — Eureka removes market-service from registry (lease-expiration-duration exceeded)
t=31s: order-service calls marketClient.getQuote():
  - Feign asks LoadBalancer for market-service instances
  - Registry shows 0 instances
  - LoadBalancer throws NoInstanceAvailableException
  - → order-service gets an error (500)
  - With Resilience4j circuit breaker (Topic 15.4): returns fallback instead

test_cases.expected:
t=0: market-service killed — stops sending heartbeats
t=10s: Eureka expects heartbeat (lease-renewal-interval) — none arrives, marks as missed
t=30s: Lease expiration — Eureka removes market-service from registry (lease-expiration-duration exceeded)
t=31s: order-service calls marketClient.getQuote():
  - Feign asks LoadBalancer for market-service instances
  - Registry shows 0 instances
  - LoadBalancer throws NoInstanceAvailableException
  - → order-service gets an error (500)
  - With Resilience4j circuit breaker (Topic 15.4): returns fallback instead

hints: Eureka doesn't know immediately — it waits for heartbeat timeout
After lease-expiration-duration: instance removed from registry
NoInstanceAvailableException: no registered instances for that service name

exercises.title: Eureka + Actuator health integration
exercises.scenario: Eureka uses Actuator health to determine instance status.
exercises.instructions:
Configure order-service so that:
1. Eureka uses the /actuator/health endpoint to determine instance health
2. If the order DB goes DOWN, Eureka marks the instance as OUT_OF_SERVICE
3. Write a custom HealthIndicator that checks DB connectivity
4. Show the Eureka/Actuator application.properties needed
hints: eureka.client.healthcheck.enabled=true: Eureka reads /actuator/health
Health.down() → Eureka marks instance OUT_OF_SERVICE
OUT_OF_SERVICE instances get no traffic — effective automatic isolation

exercises.title: Multi-profile Eureka config — dev vs prod
exercises.scenario: Dev uses local Eureka; prod uses the production registry.
exercises.instructions:
Write three property files for multi-environment Eureka:
1. `application.properties` — shared: service name, heartbeat intervals
2. `application-dev.properties` — local Eureka at localhost:8761
3. `application-prod.properties` — production Eureka at eureka.internal.company.com:8761

Also show @Profile('dev') DevDiscoveryController that logs all registered services at startup.
hints: Prod: two Eureka URLs separated by comma — high availability
Prod: longer lease intervals (30s/90s) to reduce churn
@Profile('dev') DevDiscoveryController: only in dev, never in prod

exercises.title: Service registry client — build your own discovery endpoint
exercises.scenario: Expose a /registry endpoint that shows all services and instances.
exercises.instructions:
Build a `RegistryController` in the Eureka-aware admin service:
1. GET /admin/registry → map of {serviceName: [instances]} for all services
2. GET /admin/registry/{service} → detailed info: host, port, status, metadata
3. GET /admin/registry/{service}/count → count of UP instances only
4. Uses DiscoveryClient and ServiceInstance

Test with @SpringBootTest.
hints: discoveryClient.getInstances('name'): List<ServiceInstance>
instance.getUri(): http://host:port
instance.getMetadata(): Map<String,String> custom metadata from client

exercises.title: Instance metadata — pass environment info through Eureka
exercises.scenario: Services can register custom metadata (version, region, color for blue-green).
exercises.instructions:
Configure order-service to register with custom metadata:
- version: 2.0.1
- environment: production
- datacenter: ap-south-1

Write a `SmartLoadBalancerFilter` that prefers instances with matching version metadata. Show the application.properties for metadata.
hints: eureka.instance.metadata-map.KEY=VALUE: register custom metadata
instance.getMetadata().get('version'): read metadata from a discovered instance
This powers blue-green deployment, canary releases, version routing

exercises.title: Complete Zerodha trading platform — Eureka integrated
exercises.scenario: Full microservices platform with Eureka discovery, no hardcoded URLs.
exercises.instructions:
Build the complete Zerodha trading platform with Eureka:
1. `eureka-server` (port 8761): @EnableEurekaServer, full config
2. `market-service` (port 8081): @EnableDiscoveryClient, stock quote endpoint
3. `order-service` (port 8082): @EnableDiscoveryClient + @EnableFeignClients, MarketServiceClient (no url=)
4. `TradeService.executeTrade()`: uses Feign to discover market-service
5. Show all three application.properties
6. Write test for order-service with Eureka disabled (use @MockBean MarketServiceClient)

All compile. Test passes.
hints: eureka.client.enabled=false: disables Eureka completely for tests
@MockBean MarketServiceClient: replaces Feign proxy with Mockito mock
Test verifies business logic without any network calls
